did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically self-defined CNN, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, and VGG-16, to classify seven major skin cancer types using the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed Natural Language Processing (NLP) with TF-IDF for a diagnostic robot system interaction.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and comparison of five CNN models (self-defined, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, VGG-16) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer classification, along with an NLP-based diagnostic robot system. It reports specific training/testing accuracy metrics and loss values derived from their own experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: CNN Implementation on Major Skin Cancer Types Classification and NLP Diagnose Robot System
journal: 2021 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Computer Engineering (ICAICE)
brief_summary: This study developed a skin cancer classification system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to diagnose seven major skin cancer types from the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers implemented and compared five models: a self-defined CNN, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, and VGG-16, utilizing TF-IDF for an accompanying Natural Language Processing diagnostic robot. Evaluation metrics included training and testing accuracy and loss over 25 epochs. The results indicated that ResNet-18 achieved the highest testing accuracy of 0.8995, outperforming the other models. The self-defined CNN followed with a testing accuracy of 0.8237, while LeNet5, AlexNet, and VGG-16 yielded lower accuracies of 0.4857, 0.4715, and 0.7544, respectively. The study concludes that ResNet-18 provides the best performance for this specific skin cancer classification task.

all_task_types.target: Seven major skin cancer types (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a skin cancer classification system using CNNs (self-defined, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, VGG-16) on the HAM10000 dataset to predict discrete diagnostic categories. The model outputs probabilities for each class, and the final prediction is determined by argmax. The task is explicitly described as classifying seven different kinds of skin cancer.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 10,015 dermatologic common pigmented skin lesion images acquired from different populations for training purposes. The images are labeled with specific skin positions, genders, and ages. The dataset is imbalanced, with melanocytic nevi (nv) being the majority class.

use.training: Used for training five CNN models (self-defined, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, VGG-16). Data was balanced using SMOTE, processed to 28x28 pixels with triple color channels, and split into trainable and test arrays.
use.validation: Validation accuracy and loss were reported for the models (e.g., ResNet-18 val_accuracy 0.8995), implying a validation split was used during training, though specific counts are not reported.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. Testing accuracy and loss were reported for all five models (e.g., ResNet-18 test accuracy 0.8995).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated and compared using descriptive metrics such as training accuracy, validation accuracy, testing accuracy, and loss values.

HAM10000.experiment: Skin Cancer Classification (7 classes) with 25 epochs
HAM10000.notes: ResNet-18 achieved the highest testing accuracy (0.8995). The paper notes that LeNet5 had the lowest accuracy due to its basic structure. AlexNet performed better than LeNet5. ResNet showed good performance but exhibited overfitting in validation results. VGG-16 had high test accuracy but deeper network structure. The dataset was balanced using SMOTE.

architecture_used.main_model: Five models were implemented and compared: Self-defined CNN, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, and VGG-16.
architecture_used.input_size: 28x28 pixels with triple color channels (784 pixels total).

Self-defined CNN.description: Custom architecture using convolutional layers, a flattening layer to connect to dense output layers, and dropout layers to decrease overfitting. The structure includes six layers: three dense layers each followed by a dropout layer, ending with one dense output layer.
Self-defined CNN.layers: Convolutional layers, flattening layer, three dense layers (each followed by dropout), one final dense output layer.

LeNet5.description: Proposed by Yann LeCun in 1998. Uses gradient-based backpropagation. Converts original image into feature maps through alternately connected convolutional and down-sampling (pooling) layers, passed to a fully connected neural network.
LeNet5.activation: Not explicitly specified in text, but standard LeNet uses sigmoid/tanh; paper notes AlexNet replaced Sigmoid with ReLU, implying LeNet may have used Sigmoid or similar, but not explicitly stated.

AlexNet.description: Improves LeNet5 by deepening the network. Replaces Sigmoid with ReLU activation function. Introduces Dropout to suppress overfitting. Uses data augmentation (inversion, random cropping).

VGG-16.description: 16 layers total: 13 convolutional layers and 3 fully-connected layers. Uses consecutive small convolution kernels. Structure: Two convolutions of 64 kernels + pooling; two convolutions of 128 kernels + pooling; two convolutions of 512 kernels + pooling; two fully connected layers with 4096 nodes; one fully connected layer with 1000 nodes.

ResNet-18.description: Deep residual network designed to solve vanishing gradient problems in deep networks. Uses residual blocks where weights are superimposed and activation function is passed after sub-weights are superimposed. Input signal and output are superimposed before passing through activation function.

training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy loss (calculated by loss function [11] in text, formula provided: loss = - sum(y_i * log(y_hat_i))).
training_details.data_preprocessing: Data balanced using SMOTE (imblearn library). Images reshaped to 28x28 pixels. Triple color channels used.
training_details.regularization: Dropout layers used in Self-defined CNN, AlexNet, and implied in others for overfitting control.

classification_or_output_head.final_layer: Dense layer with 7 features (nodes) for the seven disease classes.
classification_or_output_head.prediction_method: Argmax applied to the probability array to determine the class position.

preprocessing_techniques: Data balancing using SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) via the imblearn library to address class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset.
Using triple color channels (RGB) for input images.
Splitting data into trainable arrays and test arrays.
Converting input images to arrays for model input.
Using the Pillow library to convert input pictures to the desired size and format for the diagnostic robot system.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to balance the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset by generating synthetic samples for minority classes. Implemented using the imblearn library.
Applied as a data augmentation method specifically for the AlexNet model to generate new training data from the limited dataset.
Applied as a data augmentation method specifically for the AlexNet model to generate new training data from the limited dataset.

conclusions: ResNet-18 achieved the highest testing accuracy (0.8995) among the five tested models (Self-defined CNN, LeNet5, AlexNet, ResNet-18, VGG-16) for classifying seven major skin cancer types using the HAM10000 dataset.
The Self-defined CNN achieved a testing accuracy of 0.8237, while LeNet5 (0.4857), AlexNet (0.4715), and VGG-16 (0.7544) showed lower performance.
LeNet5 exhibited the lowest accuracy due to its basic network structure.
AlexNet performed better than LeNet5, attributed to the replacement of Sigmoid with ReLU activation and the introduction of Dropout to suppress overfitting.
ResNet-18 demonstrated the best training accuracy (0.9926) but showed signs of overfitting in validation results, potentially due to image format issues or Batch Specification layer problems.
VGG-16 achieved high test accuracy (0.7544) with a deeper network and smaller convolution kernels, which reduced parameters while maintaining receptive field.
The study successfully implemented a skin cancer classification system using CNNs and integrated an NLP-based diagnostic robot system using TF-IDF for patient interaction.
The authors acknowledge the overfitting problem in the ResNet model as a limitation and plan to address it in future work.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the ResNet-18 model exhibited overfitting, potentially due to incorrect image formats or Batch Specification layer issues, which is a significant limitation for clinical deployment.

The HAM10000 dataset is highly imbalanced, with melanocytic nevi (nv) comprising 6,705 out of 10,015 samples; the authors addressed this using SMOTE, but this synthetic balancing may not reflect real-world prevalence.

The input images were resized to 28x28 pixels, which is unusually low resolution for dermatological diagnosis and may discard critical visual features necessary for distinguishing between similar skin cancer types.
The paper lacks external validation; results are derived solely from the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance differences between the five CNN models.
The NLP component relies on a limited corpus and TF-IDF cosine similarity, which may not provide clinically robust or authoritative responses for complex patient inquiries.

The study is a conference paper with limited depth in methodological rigor compared to journal publications, and the authors themselves note the need for future work to address overfitting and improve the medical corpus.
